Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd just... not. Sometimes I'd have these bursts where I'd log in and watch a bunch of videos or do discussion posts, but other times whole weeks would go by where I barely touched anything. Being 18 and new to university, I really struggled to create any kind of structure for myself without someone telling me what to do.
Q2: My pattern was super inconsistent and mostly deadline-driven. I'd ignore things for days, then panic when something was due and cram everything in at once. Without regular internet access at home, I had to plan around when I could get to campus or a library, which made keeping a regular schedule really difficult. Some weeks I'd study a lot, other weeks almost nothing.
Q3: Usually it was fear, honestly. I'd get scared that I was falling behind or that I'd missed something important, and that anxiety would push me to log in. Sometimes it was because I got a notification about a deadline or saw classmates talking about an assignment. The gamified tasks actually helped because earning points or badges gave me a little dopamine hit that made logging in feel less overwhelming.
Q4: For a while, the gamified elements made checking the LMS feel almost like a habit. I'd want to see if I'd earned any new badges or where I ranked. But it never really became a true routine because my internet situation was so unpredictable. When I had access, I'd binge-use the platform, but then I'd disappear for days when I couldn't get online.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually really helpful because I learn better when I can talk things through with people. The video lectures were good too because I could pause and rewind when I didn't understand something. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so anything that let me actively do something rather than just read felt more useful to me.
Q6: I basically ignored the readings and textbook stuff because I just can't focus when I'm staring at walls of text. The exam prep materials I avoided because they stressed me out too much - just looking at them made my anxiety spike. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came until it was too late.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it was interactive. The gamified tasks were great because I got instant feedback and could see my progress. Anything that felt like busywork or didn't connect to what I actually needed to know felt like a waste, especially when I had limited time online.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. I'd think 'oh, it's optional, I can skip it' but then I'd feel guilty and anxious about not doing them. Required ones at least gave me clear direction. With optional stuff, I never knew if I was missing something important or if it was truly okay to skip, which just added to my stress.
Q9: Yes, definitely. Since I don't have reliable internet at home, I'd download everything I could when I was online and then study from those files later. I'd print things out at the library and work from paper copies. I also recorded myself reading my notes out loud so I could listen back and learn that way - very kinesthetic, you know?
Q10: So many times! I'd download a video once, but then watch it like three or four times offline while taking handwritten notes. Or I'd be pacing around my room reciting concepts out loud, acting out processes to understand them better. None of that shows up anywhere, but that's actually when I was learning the most.
Q11: All the time, that was basically my main study method. I'd grab everything during my limited online windows and then work with it offline for days. My assignment scores were actually pretty good because I'd really dig into the materials once I had them downloaded. The LMS would just show one quick access, but I'd spend hours with that content.
Q12: Around exam time, everything fell apart. My anxiety got so bad that I'd freeze up and avoid studying altogether even though I knew I needed to. The fear of failing made it impossible to actually engage with exam prep materials. Also, when I couldn't get internet access for several days, I'd fall so far behind that catching up felt impossible.
Q13: Absolutely. Not having internet at home was the biggest barrier - I'd have to plan my whole week around when I could get online. When I was stressed or anxious, I'd procrastinate by doing the easy gamified tasks instead of the hard exam studying. Fatigue was real too; sometimes I'd just be too mentally exhausted to focus on anything complex.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be logged in and scrolling through materials but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch videos but not actually absorb anything because I was too anxious about everything I still had to do. It was like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: The exam prep stuff. I knew it was crucial, but every time I tried to start, this wave of fear would wash over me and I'd do literally anything else. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, or I'd convince myself that doing more discussion posts was just as important. It was pure avoidance driven by anxiety.
Q16: Deadlines were everything. They were the only thing that could override my avoidance and anxiety. I'd cram everything into the last possible moment, which is why my assignment completion and scores were decent even though my overall engagement looked messy. Without a deadline, I'd just drift.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it actually motivated me to keep going. It was like proof that I wasn't completely failing. But feedback on exams was devastating - seeing those low scores made me want to avoid the course entirely. The gamified feedback was nice because it was always positive, always showing progress.
Q18: Having so many online courses at once was overwhelming. I was juggling 17 courses and couldn't keep track of what was due where. The structure made me feel scattered rather than focused. I needed more guidance and check-ins, not just content dumped on me to figure out myself.
Q19: The gamification made engagement easier - it gave me small wins when everything else felt like failure. But the exam-heavy grading made things harder because I'm terrible at exams even when I understand the material. Having limited resources and no internet made the online-only design really challenging for someone like me.
Q20: That's kind of me, isn't it? I think it happens when you're anxious and trying to engage but can't actually focus. You're clicking around, watching things, posting in discussions because it feels productive, but you're avoiding the hard stuff that actually counts for your grade. Fear can make you busy without being effective.
Q21: I can see how that happens - if someone has good resources, reliable internet, and studies effectively offline, their LMS activity might look low but they're actually learning. For me though, my low attendance and exam scores show that my offline studying wasn't enough to compensate for missing so much class.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're online and what you click on, but they can't show if you're actually learning or just going through motions. They miss all the offline studying, the anxiety that makes you freeze, the real understanding that happens when you're walking around thinking about concepts. They see activity, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it, not just complete it. It's when you're curious and want to know more, when studying doesn't feel like torture. For me, I was engaged with the interactive stuff and discussions, but completely disengaged from exam prep because fear blocked me.
Q24: The LMS can't show how scared I was all the time, how much I actually cared about doing well even though my grades didn't show it. It can't capture the hours I spent studying from downloaded materials or how hard it was to engage without reliable internet. My high assignment scores show I can do the work - the data just doesn't explain why exams destroyed me.
